refnamediv.refpurpose: Use smb over object store provided by ceph librgw.

refsect1.title: DESCRIPTION
refsect1.para: This VFS module is part of the samba 8 suite.
The vfs_ceph_rgw VFS module exposes ceph-rgw hosted s3 bucket to users.

Ceph is a distributed network file system designed to provide excellent performance, reliability, and scalability. librgw is shared library allowing applications to access an object store i.e. s3 bucket via a posix-like interface.

This module can not be combined with other modules.
librgw do not support renaming directories. Therefore param 'vfs mkdir use tmp name' must be set to 'no'. This would ensure samba do not use temporary names while creating directory and rename it to give name. Thus vfs module sets 'vfs mkdir use tmp name' to 'no', to ensure samba does not use temporary names for dir creation.

refsect1.title: CONFIGURATION
refsect1.para: vfs_ceph_rgw requires that the underlying share path is a s3 bucket name.

Since vfs_ceph_rgw does not require a filesystem mount, the share path is treated differently: it is interpreted as a bucket name hosted on a ceph object store on the attached Ceph cluster. In a ctdb cluster environment where ctdb manages Samba, CTDB_SAMBA_SKIP_SHARE_CHECK=yes must be configured to disable local share path checks, otherwise ctdb will not reach a healthy state.

listitem.para: Allows one to define a ceph configfile to use. Default: /etc/ceph/ceph.conf
Allows one to define a ceph keyring file to use. Default: /etc/ceph/ceph.client.admin.keyring
access key for bucket owner specified by user_id option.
secret access key for bucket owner specified by user_id option.
Bucket name to be exported as share.
Control debug logs for librgw Default: off

refsect1.title: VERSION
refsect1.para: This man page is part of version &doc.version; of the Samba suite.

refsect1.title: AUTHOR
refsect1.para: The original Samba software and related utilities were created by Andrew Tridgell. Samba is now developed by the Samba Team as an Open Source project similar to the way the Linux kernel is developed.
